name: Rapa Nui / Easter Island (Isla de Pascua / Te Pito o te Henua)
summary: Rapa Nui / Easter Island (Isla de Pascua in Spanish / Rapa Nui in the indigenous Rapa Nui language / Te Pito o te Henua 'Navel of the World' traditional Rapa Nui name) is an eastern-Polynesian mixed-system Chilean special territory located ~3,700 km west of mainland Chile and ~2,075 km southeast of Pitcairn Island — structurally distinctive globally as the most remote permanently-inhabited island on Earth, as the only eastern-Polynesian indigenous-titular jurisdiction within a non-Polynesian state framework (Chile, since Chilean annexation 1888), and as the central jurisdiction of the indigenous-titular Rapa Nui Statute of 2018 (Ley 21.070) which established Rapa Nui's first formal indigenous-special-territory framework including a unique permanent-resident-only land-ownership regime, a population-control framework limiting inbound migration, and a Rapa Nui Council of Elders (Honui) consultation framework. Rapa Nui is administered by Chile as part of Valparaíso Region's Isla de Pascua Province (formally a Provincia, but with sui generis administrative framework under the 2018 Statute). Rapa Nui is also a UNESCO World Heritage Site (inscribed 1995) recognising its globally-significant moai monumental statuary heritage. Family-law framework operates under a dual Chilean Civil Code (Código Civil 1855) + Rapa Nui customary-law-recognition framework via Indigenous Law 19.253 of 1993 (Ley Indígena) which recognises Rapa Nui as one of nine officially-recognised indigenous peoples in Chile. Parental authority and child custody operate under Chilean Civil Code Articles 222-244 (patria potestad / cuidado personal) with Indigenous Law 19.253 cultural-recognition framework. Regional courts of Valparaíso Region have jurisdiction over Rapa Nui matters; final appellate jurisdiction lies with the Supreme Court of Chile. Rapa Nui is silent on 'parental alienation' as a statutory label. Chile is a Hague Convention 1980 party (acceded effective 1 May 1994) — Rapa Nui Hague applicability via Chilean territorial extension.

statutory_framework.citation: Ley 21.070 Estatuto Especial para Rapa Nui (Rapa Nui Statute) 2018
statutory_framework.title: Rapa Nui Statute 2018
statutory_framework.relevance: Chilean Law of 25 March 2018 establishing Rapa Nui's first formal indigenous-special-territory framework — permanent-resident-only land-ownership regime, population-control framework, Honui Council of Elders consultation framework.

statutory_framework.title: Chilean Indigenous Law
statutory_framework.relevance: Chilean Law of 28 September 1993 recognising Rapa Nui as one of nine officially-recognised indigenous peoples in Chile — Rapa Nui customary-law-recognition framework.

statutory_framework.citation: Chilean Civil Code 1855 (applicable in Rapa Nui)
statutory_framework.title: Chilean Civil Code
statutory_framework.relevance: Chilean Civil Code Articles 222-244 governing patria potestad and cuidado personal applicable in Rapa Nui via Valparaíso Region administrative framework.

statutory_framework.title: Treaty of Cession 1888
statutory_framework.relevance: International instrument of 9 September 1888 transferring sovereignty over Rapa Nui to Chile — Chilean position: full cession; Rapa Nui Council of Elders position: limited cession preserving customary land rights.

apex_courts.name: Court of Appeals of Valparaíso (Corte de Apelaciones de Valparaíso)
apex_courts.role: Apex regional court for civil and criminal matters from Rapa Nui.

apex_courts.name: Supreme Court of Chile (Corte Suprema)
apex_courts.role: Final appellate court for civil and criminal matters from Rapa Nui.

professional_regulators.name: Colegio de Psicólogos de Chile
professional_regulators.role: Chilean professional regulatory framework for psychology applicable in Rapa Nui.

anonymisation_convention: Rapa Nui-related decisions are anonymised per Chilean court practice using initials.

key_developments.title: First European contact + Jacob-Roggeveen-Easter-Day framework
key_developments.description: First European contact 5 April 1722 by Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen on Easter Day — substantively distinctive globally Easter-Day-naming-+-only-eastern-Polynesian-island-with-Dutch-first-contact framework. Foundational pre-1888-Chilean-annexation framework affecting subsequent constitutional-administrative-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Peruvian slave raids + demographic-collapse framework
key_developments.description: Peruvian slave raids 1862-1863 + smallpox/tuberculosis epidemics substantively reshaping demographic framework — substantively distinctive globally demographic-collapse-from-3000-to-111-in-15-years framework. Substantive demographic and family-law-implementation disruption affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory and Rapa Nui-indigenous-population framework.

key_developments.title: Chilean annexation + Treaty-of-Cession-+-Acuerdo-de-Voluntades framework
key_developments.description: Treaty of Cession (Acuerdo de Voluntades) 9 September 1888 transferring sovereignty over Rapa Nui to Chile — substantively distinctive globally Chilean-position-full-cession-vs-Rapa-Nui-Council-of-Elders-position-limited-cession-preserving-customary-land-rights framework. Foundational substantive sovereignty-interpretation-dispute framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Chilean Civil Code + civil-law-substantive-heritage framework
key_developments.description: Chilean Civil Code (Código Civil) enacted 1855 + applicable to Rapa Nui from 1888 annexation — substantively significant Chilean-civil-law-substantive-heritage framework. Foundational substantive civil-law framework persisting through subsequent reform-trajectory affecting family-law-implementation including arts. 222-244 governing patria potestad and cuidado personal.

key_developments.title: Easter Island Act + Ley-Pascua + Chilean-civil-status framework
key_developments.description: Chilean Easter Island Act (Ley 16.441 Ley Pascua) 28 January 1966 — substantively distinctive Polynesian ending-pre-1966-colonial-company-administration framework. Substantive integration into Valparaíso Province with full Chilean civil-status framework. Foundational pre-1993-Indigenous-Law framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Chile ratifies UN Convention on the Rights of the Child + Rapa-Nui-applicability framework
key_developments.description: Chile ratified UNCRC 13 August 1990 with applicability to Rapa Nui — establishing best-interests-of-the-child substantive doctrine within Chilean-derivative-civil-law framework applicable to Rapa Nui.

key_developments.title: Ley Indígena + Chilean-Indigenous-Law + nine-recognised-indigenous-peoples framework
key_developments.description: Chilean Law 19.253 of 28 September 1993 (Ley Indígena) recognising Rapa Nui as one of nine officially-recognised indigenous peoples in Chile — substantively significant Polynesian Rapa-Nui-customary-law-recognition framework. Foundational substantive indigenous-law framework affecting subsequent reform-trajectory affecting family-law-implementation.

key_developments.title: Chilean Hague Convention 1980 accession + Rapa-Nui-applicability framework
key_developments.description: Chile acceded to Hague Convention 1980 effective 1 May 1994 with applicability to Rapa Nui via Chilean territorial extension — substantively significant Polynesian Hague-applicability framework. Substantive cross-border-jurisdiction-practice framework affecting subsequent reform-trajectory.

key_developments.title: UNESCO World Heritage Site inscription + Rapa-Nui-National-Park-+-moai framework
key_developments.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site inscription of Rapa Nui National Park 1995 — substantively distinctive globally moai-monumental-statuary-heritage framework. Substantive Polynesian heritage-preservation framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory and population-control framework.

key_developments.title: Rapa Nui Statute + Ley-21.070-+-permanent-resident-only-land-+-Honui-Council framework
key_developments.description: Chilean Law 21.070 (Estatuto Especial para Rapa Nui) 25 March 2018 establishing Rapa Nui's first formal indigenous-special-territory framework — substantively distinctive globally only-modern-Chilean-indigenous-special-territory-framework-with-permanent-resident-only-land-ownership-regime-+-population-control-framework framework. Honui Council of Elders consultation framework. Foundational substantive Rapa Nui-Statute framework affecting subsequent constitutional-democratic-trajectory.

key_developments.title: Boric government + INDH + Honui-Council-continuing-consultation + COVID-19 post-pandemic-tourism-reopening framework
key_developments.description: Gabriel Boric (Apruebo Dignidad/Convergencia Social) Presidency framework continuing through 2023 with Instituto Nacional de Derechos Humanos (INDH) institutional positions addressing PA-related issues within Chilean Civil Code 1855 arts. 222-244 + Indigenous Law 19.253 + Rapa Nui Statute 2018 framework. Continuing Honui Council of Elders consultation framework substantively significant institutional context. COVID-19 post-pandemic Rapa Nui tourism reopening framework August 2022+ substantively significant institutional context affecting Rapa Nui demographic-and-cultural-preservation framework. Substantively significant eastern-Polynesian Chilean-special-territory institutional jurisprudential development.

structural_findings: Rapa Nui operates a mixed Chilean civil-law + Rapa Nui customary-law-recognition framework — places Rapa Nui in the eastern-Polynesian Chilean-special-territory cluster.
Most remote permanently-inhabited island on Earth is structurally distinctive globally — ~3,700 km from mainland Chile, ~2,075 km from nearest neighbour Pitcairn.
Only eastern-Polynesian indigenous-titular jurisdiction within a non-Polynesian state framework is structurally distinctive globally.

2018 Rapa Nui Statute (Ley 21.070) is structurally distinctive globally — only modern Chilean indigenous-special-territory framework with permanent-resident-only land-ownership regime and population-control framework.
Honui Council of Elders consultation framework is structurally distinctive within Chilean administrative cluster.
UNESCO World Heritage Site moai monumental statuary heritage is structurally distinctive globally.
Chilean Indigenous Law 19.253 recognition is shared with Mapuche, Aymara, and six other Chilean indigenous peoples but distinctive in Pacific-Polynesian framework.

Chilean Hague Convention 1980 accession 1994 + Rapa Nui Statute population-control framework intersection is structurally distinctive — modern Hague-Convention-applicability vs population-control framework intersection.

sources.title: Biblioteca del Congreso Nacional de Chile (BCN)
UNESCO World Heritage Centre — Rapa Nui National Park

editorial_notes: Rapa Nui / Easter Island jurisdiction sidecar v1.1 — deepened 2026-06-09 from 5 to 10 key_developments with full Roggeveen-first-contact-1722-to-Rapa-Nui-Statute-2018 trajectory: 1722-first-European-contact-+-Jacob-Roggeveen-Easter-Day + 1862-Peruvian-slave-raids-+-demographic-collapse + 1855-Chilean-Civil-Code + 1888-Chilean-annexation-+-Treaty-of-Cession + 1966-Easter-Island-Act-Ley-Pascua + 1990-Chile-UNCRC-ratification + 1993-Ley-Indígena + 1994-Chilean-Hague-Convention-accession + 1995-UNESCO-World-Heritage + 2018-Rapa-Nui-Statute-Ley-21.070.

Mixed Chilean civil-law/Rapa Nui customary-law-recognition eastern-Polynesian Chilean special territory (Chilean Civil Code 1855 + Indigenous Law 19.253 1993 + Rapa Nui Statute 2018 (Ley 21.070) permanent-resident-only land-ownership regime + Honui Council of Elders consultation framework + UNESCO World Heritage 1995 + 1888 Treaty of Cession + Chilean Hague Convention 1980 accession 1994). Most remote permanently-inhabited island on Earth globally + only eastern-Polynesian indigenous-titular jurisdiction within non-Polynesian state framework + only modern Chilean indigenous-special-territory framework with permanent-resident-only land-ownership regime and population-control framework + moai monumental statuary UNESCO heritage + demographic collapse from 3,000 to 111 in 15 years (1862-1877) historically distinctive.

PA-recognition: silent statutory + no-apex-position + silent regulator — substantive best-interests-of-the-child analysis under Chilean Civil Code 1855 arts. 222-244 + Indigenous Law 19.253 1993 + Rapa Nui Statute 2018 + Hague Convention 1980 framework without doctrinal 'parental alienation' label.

Joins eastern-Polynesian + mixed-Chilean-civil-law/Rapa-Nui-customary-recognition + Chilean-special-territory-cluster + most-remote-permanently-inhabited-island-on-Earth-globally-distinctive-3700km-from-mainland-Chile + only-eastern-Polynesian-indigenous-titular-jurisdiction-within-non-Polynesian-state-framework + Rapa-Nui-Statute-2018-Ley-21.070-permanent-resident-only-land-ownership-+-population-control-globally-distinctive + Honui-Council-of-Elders-consultation + UNESCO-moai-monumental-statuary-heritage-1995 + Indigenous-Law-19.253-1993-nine-recognised-indigenous-peoples + Roggeveen-Easter-Day-naming-1722 + Peruvian-slave-raids-1862-1863-demographic-collapse + Chilean-annexation-1888-+-Treaty-of-Cession-disputed-interpretation + Easter-Island-Act-Ley-Pascua-1966 + Chilean-Hague-1994-accession clusters within the corpus.
